comments.content: Several Magic Mondays ago, you commented that ancestor veneration was fine as long as one didn't try to summon the dead. Is there a difference between summoning and merely inviting or welcoming? Also, is it okay to perform a banishing ritual in the same location as one's ancestor altar?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, there's a difference between summoning and welcoming. In the latter, you're just inviting your ancestors to be present in the way that they are normally present. In the former, you're trying to force a bridge between the realms of the dead and the living so that the dead will be present in a way that they're not normally present.

2) Yes, that would be a good idea, in fact, because it will keep anything unwanted from hanging around your ancestor altar.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Altar
replies.content:
1) A little Florida water would do a good job. You might also see if your ancestors are good with a bowl of pure salt on the altar -- that's a Japanese custom for altars, and it absorbs a lot of crud.

2) Low-grade predatory or parasitic spirits looking to get in on whatever you offer to your ancestors. If you get a "creepy" feeling around the altar, that's a common sign. Salt them as though they were slugs!

Dear JMG,

1. On the open post this week, a commenter asked about positive evil in the undisciplined person, and you responded that an undisciplined doesn't realize "that a goal pursued via evil means inevitably takes on the evil itself."

I've not read Fortune before so I hope I'm not misunderstanding the concept, but I wanted to check whether a situation I'm in corresponds to it. I recently took on a promotion to low level management at the company I work at. I've also been working through the DMH and been practicing daily the SOP, meditation, divination, etc.

The job has pros and cons but frankly, I don't know what else I can do to earn a living, so I have justified it by applying what I learn in the DMH to my job - doing it well as a kind of practical application of my spiritual practice by being attentive, not treating my job as something to resent, etc.

But I already find myself using the de-humanizing language of management (referring to people I manage as 'resources' during management meetings, etc) simply because that's the lingo that the other managers use, so I'm worried this might be positive evil starting to influence my thought processes. This type of abstract language seems somehow counter to the philosophy of the DMH in that it 'de-enchants' things and people into cogs in business processes. But maybe I'm reading too much into it.

Do you think that this is the case, ie, have I understood positive evil correctly? If so, how can i protect myself from being corrupted in this way?

2. I just received the Dolmen Arch in the mail (it is a beautiful-looking book). A few questions about it:

a) Is it 'cheating' to read it fully first, ie, are you not supposed to read ahead until doing the exercises in a chapter?

b) On the open post, you pointed out the difference between a mage, mystic and occultist. The Dolmen Arch looks to be an occultist work, designed to help cultivate wisdom, is that right?

c) I'm about halfway through the DMH after about a year, and as I go through it, I realize that my interest is essentially working to create the lunar current in myself, rather than enchanting objects or doing magic for practical purposes. I understood that the lunar current is related to the soul and its powers, and to enlightenment. In terms of the terminology you set out (mysticism, magic, and occultism), is creating the lunar current the same as the quest for wisdom?

d) I'd still like to finish the DMH, but I took it on originally ignorant of the distinction between occultism and operative magic, and now I'm pretty sure self-development, getting in touch with the higher self, and learning to perhaps one day actually a bit wiser than I am now, are my real goals. It looks like the Dolmen Arch is tailored for that. If that's true - can I do the Dolmen Arch and the DMH at the same time, and if so, do you have suggestions about how best to do so? It looks like the issue would be the need for multiple sessions of discursive meditation per day, because there's so much to meditate on in both systems.

Thanks as always!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, but I'm in a similar situation and have a couple ideas that are working for me.

I never wanted to be a manager where I work, but had it thrust upon me 18 months ago with no option. The corporate culture is for managers to refer to things as "my program" and people as "my staff". Even people who came up through the ranks, so to speak, change to that verbiage almost immediately. I find this to be egotistical and dehumanizing.

So, what I've done is to remember to put myself in the shoes of the other team members in the group I've been told to lead. I've asked both of them to quickly call me out on it if I revert to the corporate normal wording. I've asked them to call my wife and tell her and that she'll beat the living whatsis out of me if I get outside of the bounds of being a human team leader. I also slow down and think through what I'm saying if it is related to our work group and involves the other team members. Notice the way I've referred to the other members of the work group that I have been appointed to lead: the wording may appear awkward, but it keeps me away from the dehumanizing corporate norm. It is "our" group and "our" program.

Also, I used an affirmation for several months that was very personalized for my situation. It helped a lot.

DJSpo

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You're in a situation in which you can do a great deal to make the lives of your subordinates less miserable. DJSpo's advice just above is well worth following as a way to do that. You can also use this situation as a venue for training the will and imagination. Affirmations are helpful here; so is the practice of attending to what you say and how you say it, and making a conscious choice to use language that includes and empowers the people who work with you.

2) You can certainly read it; yes, the Dolmen Arch is a textbook of Druidical occultism; yes, the awakening of the lunar current is another way of talking about the quest for wisdom; and the Dolmen Arch and the Druid Magic Handbook systems are designed to be interchangeable and interconvertible -- that is, they form between them a broader system from which the practitioner can draw out those parts that fit his or her own needs. (This is Druidry, after all, which means that there ain't no such thing as One True Path.) Thus my suggestion is to maintain the basic practices -- the SoP and Grail Working, discursive meditation, and daily divination -- and decide which of the two systems of training makes the most sense for you at this point in your life. (You can always go back and do the other one later on, you know...)

Many religious and philosophical traditions, among them Buddhism, Gnosticism, and to some degree Christianity (possibly Hinduism as well, but I'm less familiar with it), conceive of the material world as an error, illusion, or fundamentally unsatisfactory, a place of sin and suffering, and make it the goal of human life to transcend it and achieve a perfect spiritual state beyond matter (consider, for example, the Buddhist description of this world as dukkha, or the Christian description of it as a vale of tears). Often, my own life has seemed to me like a punishment, a harsh learning experience of some kind, a making amends for previous faults and mistakes (and this is while living a fairly privileged life in the upper middle class of a first world democracy), so it’s hard to shake off the idea that I am here to be tested, in preparation for rejoining a higher state of union.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, I don't think there is merit to those views -- in fact, you've just touched on what I consider to be the essential failure of the prophetic religions. All of them start off by positing an arbitrary concept of what humanity and the universe ought to be like, and then denounce humanity and the universe because neither one lives up to expectations. The resulting cognitive dissonance runs all through the prophetic faiths, with any number of unpleasant and/or embarrassing consequences.

The problem of evil, in effect, is only a problem to those who postulate a false idea of what the universe is and then have to explain away the inaccuracies that result. It seems far saner to me to accept that the world is what it is, and to avoid letting our concepts of spiritual reality be held hostage to an overdeveloped sense of entitlement.

One other thing -- it's actually quite common for people who lead relatively privileged lives to become convinced that the world is a horrible place and ought to be replaced with one better suited to their preferences. The Buddha started out life as a pampered princeling, remember!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: But it seems in that case that we're "held hostage" by a universe that we had no choice in shaping, at least when it comes to the fundamental tenets of existence (obviously, we each have some say in shaping our relationship to others, community, society, etc.) Humans are endowed with the faculties of consciousness, free will, and moral discernment, and I find no real entitlement in saying "No, the current state of affairs isn't acceptable to me. I want out." Accepting the universe as-is is no more justified than rejecting it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Hmm. Thinking that the universe ought to conform to your notions of what is and is not acceptable strikes me as a sense of entitlement on a truly cosmic scale. The universe follows its own laws and has its own concerns; it considers your wants about as much as you consider the wants of the bacteria on your skin; you can accept that and figure out how to make the most of your existence, or you can throw a more or less philosophically refined tantrum, which the universe will not notice and which will do nothing to improve your life or anyone else's. That's how I see it, at least.

The peasant boy down the lane, who grew up learning to enjoy a plain bowl of rice and chickpeas as much as Siddhartha Gautama enjoyed his lavish feasts, and who got to know all about sickness, age, and death from an early age by being a member of an ordinary human community, didn't have that problem.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
JMG, a funny little thought just arose: A Buddhist monk friend of mine would probably label your views as "pro-Samara" or some similar pejorative snipe. This reminds me of how much not-so-acknowledged crossover there is between Buddhism and Christianity.

Myself being of something approximate to the Neoplatonist persuasion, believe there are plenty of realms of higher existence where most of the troubles and sometimes horrors of the material don't really exist, and that humans do have the capacity to mature beyond the cycle of rebirths in the material.

But yeah, I do see your point in that basing an entire religion on the idea that the material realm is evil or illusory or awash in sin or whatever, is just setting up the vast majority of people for failure and such a doctrine gets easily used/abused as a form of psychological warfare against the masses. I think many schools of Hinduism handle this issue with more grace and care; the samsara doctrine exists but most people aren't expected to become ascetics and devote their lives to enlightenment; for the masses, much emphasis is placed on living an upright and dutiful here on the material plane, with the hope of accumulating enough karma for a rebirth conducive to enlightenment-seeking.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've always been impressed by Plotinus' discussion of the issue in his essay against the Gnostics. Of course there are other modes of being into which our souls can enter when they've earned the privilege, but we get there by accepting the discipline of this stage of existence, learning its lessons, and developing the strengths and capacities we need to enter the next stage up. With regard to Hinduism, I'm much taken with the concept, found in some schools, of lila -- the idea that the universe is play, a dance of the divine Parabrahm that has no point or purpose, no goal outside itself. It's like a child dreaming a world into being for no reason other than the pleasure of doing it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
JMG, I'm not sure that I agree with your position here (I may be misunderstanding you, of course, which is quite likely).

That the prophetic religions envisage an ideal world and try to make this world match up, I can see. Buddhism, though, simply accepts that evil and suffering exist. It doesn't try to change the world, or establish some ideal society; it teaches that the key to happiness lies within each of us, and in our management of our own response to the world. The Buddha didn't go off the rails; rather, it was the shock of his exposure to suffering, after being shielded from it from birth, which motivated him to find a solution which would work for everyone.

I find the Buddhist view that each of us is responsible for finding the end to our own suffering very compatible, though not the same, as the Druidic view that this world is Abred, the stage in spiritual development in which the soul uses the human capacity of thought and free will to learn from experience, including suffering, to overcome the demands of incarnate existence...

Bogatyr

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Buddhism is philosophically a great deal subtler than most other prophetic religions -- the extraordinary richness of Indian thought made that inevitable, since Buddhism had to compete with a great many other philosophically complex beliefs over the course of its history -- and so it doesn't define its notional alternative to the world we actually inhabit. It simply claims (in the third Noble Truth) that suffering can cease once and for all, and posits a state (Nirvana) which is defined only negatively as being without suffering. As the Zen master said, though, "Now that you have become enlightened, you can expect to be just as miserable as ever"; the state of being free of all suffering remains as elusive as its Christian equivalent, the state of being free of all sin.

That's why I find Taoism far more attractive among Asian teachings. Lao Tso shreds the basic assumptions of Buddhism in the second chapter of the Tao Te Ching:

"Under heaven all can see beauty as beauty only because there is ugliness.
All can know good as good only because there is evil."

That is to say, the idea of bliss without suffering is as absurd as having a coin with only one side. As I noted to another questioner earlier, the only way to avoid having a bad day now and then is to stop existing altogether; in the same way, the only way to kill out suffering is to kill out the capacity to feel, which means giving up joy as well. That doesn't strike me as very useful. Rather, accepting that suffering and joy are inescapably paired, that your capacity to experience one is equal to your capacity to experience the other, and learning to be relatively calm about it all, seems more productive to me. That said, of course, your mileage may vary!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Erm.... Well, we have very different takes on Buddhism, then! I know you've said before that your direct experience of Buddhism, of the Japanese Shingon variety. I don't know anything about that, so can't comment. I've studied mostly in the Theravadan tradition and Korean Son, more in the former, and their take on existence, good and evil, bears no resemblance to what you're describing. Anyway, this isn't really the place for discussions on Buddhist cosmology, so let's leave it there!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
But this is precisely where (IMHO) the Buddha "went of the rails." Having had no first-hand experience of suffering for so long, when he first encountered it as a young man, he saw at once that it was extremely unpleasant and quite hard to take, that it can maim or kill the soul as well as the body. From this he quite wrongly concluded that suffering was also extremely undesirable, a thing well worth avoiding, and he set about finding a way to avoid it.

However, it is just because suffering is so hard to take that it is such an excellent teacher of wisdom. For those whom it does not break and destroy in the process, it leads to deep insights that can be won in no other way.

(The other half of this eternal dynamic is expressed in Ian MacLaren's simple dictum: "Be kind, for everyone is fighting a hard battle.")

comments.title: The impact of SoP
comments.content:
This is a data point rather than a question; you asked earlier about interactions between arts such as Aikido and the SoP. I've been practicing Aikido irregularly for slightly less than 35 years. I've moved around a lot in my career and I've rarely managed to stay with one particular dojo or style for more than a year or two. That said, it's had a positive impact on me, and on the single occasion I ever had to use it 'in real life' it had an impact on somebody else. I'm not practicing at the moment though.

Shortly after Christmas this year I decide to learn the SoP but whereas I have found most arts and skills that interest me fall into my grasp quite easily - this one has been extraordinarily difficult. The combination of speech, visualisation, and gesture, performed simultaneously gives me a sensation akin to rubbing my belly and patting my head - except I've never had any problem doing that. I have yet manage a performance to my own satisfaction and after around 10 weeks I've only managed to reach Fire. The usual problem is that I have found it hard to memorize the invocations - I can do them perfectly in my head, and once or twice in dreams. Actually doing them often leave me tongue tied or leaving out a phrase. I'm becoming quite amused with my klutziness.
I've persisted, partly because I'm doing this as an ongoing project rather than a goal that I can achieve and then give up on, but mostly because I've simultaneously noticed that the unhealthy amounts of coffee that I drink daily have dwindled from 5 or more strong espressos to a single cup first thing. Even odder, my ongoing obsession with Twitter - that could occupy me for 3 or 4 hours of an evening - simply vanished over the space of a fortnight.

The idea that I was going to stop simply popped into my head one evening, apropos to nothing, and that was that. As a matter of fact I glanced at my feed for about a minute this last weekend before I noticed that it was mostly people whining and that I had better things to do. To put that into perspective, I spent stupid amounts of time watching people do little else over Brexit for the last 3 years on Twitter.
So I've no idea if I'm finding SoP hard because it's is actually quite hard, because I've spend a fair amount of time on the mat practicing Aikido, or because I've actually got a lot of crud to deal with, but it does seem to be doing something.

Andy

replies.title: Re: The impact of SoP
replies.content: Andy, many thanks for the data points! You're not the only one who's had banishing rituals banish the desire to waste time online, for what it's worth.

comments.title: The Fear of the Lord

Dear JMG,

In your other blog I noticed you recently fielded a question by someone there named "Waffles" regarding their fear of eternal damnation. I also suffer from this fear at times. More in the past than now. I've tackled it in therapy. I've tackled it by reading the higher critics. Do you have any magical recommendations to those of us raised to be afraid? I'm nearly 40 now and this is ridiculous! Also, very related, if you're willing to answer a two-part question, what is the view of magic as regards morality? Are we judged and tormented for not toeing some set of behavioral proscriptions by some magical analog of Jehovah after death if we're not good boys and girls? Book recommendations on this or related topics would be very welcome!

Sincere thanks,
Trepidation Nation

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord
replies.content: Not JMG, but I spent nearly two decades in the same boat as you - fear of hell controlled almost every decision I made. I was able to overcome it by looking into the arguments for Christian Universalism (the belief that hell, if it exists, is temporary and restorative, not eternal and retributive), and finding them utterly more convincing than the arguments for the more traditional Eternal Conscious Torment view - despite the fact that I'm no longer a Christian. Your mileage may vary, but I wanted to throw in my two cents in case it could be of any help to you. Best of luck!

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord
replies.content: Thank you for responding. I actually did look into Christian universalism quite a bit when I was first trying to cope with my fear. I found many of the arguments convincing. However, at the same time, because many of those arguments weakened the idea of hell by questioning the language used and the meanings of the language, and also the various ways that doctrines were built up over time, I began to question the whole religion! It sent me to the higher critics and then on to the mythicists. Now, despite the fact that I can wield rational arguments against the faith, it's my *emotional* brain that can't quite get the message. I think this is likely the case for many others - we were conditioned emotionally as children to be afraid. And, now, I can't use the highly verbal, cerebral aspects of my brain to alter the earlier, more instinctive aspects. It's like having a college professor give a lecture to a child in a crib about the virtues of cloth diapers vs. disposable diapers. It just doesn't sink in! I figure a magical act might be more therapeutic for this reason - it might have the finesse to get down in there in the primitive parts of my brain stem and break me (and others) free of these kind of fear-shackles.

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord
replies.content:
I saw Waffle's post as well and I'm glad he/she posted it because it gave me a great deal of insight as to what some Christians are brought up to believe. I found Nestorian's response irritating, as he immediately suggested Waffles go from the frying pan into the fire of his "One True Religion", a.k.a Nestorian Christianity.

JMG promotes an exercise (he may have invented it, I'm not sure) where you go outside, find a healthy, medium-aged tree such as a pine or an oak, and then silently ask the tree permission to exchange energy. If the vibe from the tree feels right, it is effectively a "yes" and you sit with your back to the tree and observe the effects on the way you feel. Obviously thank the tree afterwards. I think this tree exercise would help any Christian who has spent quality time fearing hell and damnation, because it opens a conversation with an invisible yet entirely natural force that is not you and is not Jesus or the angry aspect of his father. Of course talking to a tree is not talking to a god, but it's good practice for making invisible friends who are as real as you and me.

From my own two years of Druid immersion and two-way dialogues with gods, spirits, angels, and so forth, I have formed the opinion that most Christians profoundly misunderstand and accidentally pervert the god/human relationship and end up entirely shut out of it. My opinion follows, and this is only my opinion: if you can think of a god as you would any other mentor, and present yourself as an earnest student of what they know instead of assuming they'll punish you no matter what you do, that is the first step. Maybe you need to approach a god outside of the Jesus sphere to do that? I don't know. The gods have to get through some fairly thick walls of fear and self-importance to talk to talk to a human who has a lifelong fear of hell, but if you keep trying, so will they. Best of luck, and if you want me to pray for you to have an easier time communicating with gods, just say the word and I'll do that.

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord
replies.content:

I gladly accept your offering of prayer. Please do! - And thank you *very* much for offering. I also appreciate your suggestion about the trees. I will give it a go!

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord
replies.content:
I'm not really sure how much help I can offer here, since I didn't grow up Christian and so wasn't exposed to the notion of eternal damnation until I was old enough to look at it and see how absurd it is. If a human father claimed to love his children, but punished them for even minor mistakes by dragging them down into the basement and burning their skin with lit cigarettes, not just once but for the rest of their lives, I think most people would dismiss the claim that he loved his children and would consider him an abusive monster who should spend the rest of his life behind bars. The same logic applies to gods -- and fortunately, there's no reason to think that the power that creates and sustains the universe is that sort of malign thug.

As for magic, dear gods, no. From the perspective of magic, each of us lives many lives -- this isn't simply a belief, because magical practices quite routinely lead to remembering past lives. The things we experience in life are the consequence of our own previous actions, not because somebody judges us, but because our actions shape our character and our character draws our future experiences to us. As for the gods, they have better things to do than sit there peering down on us, waiting for someone to make a mistake so they can throw a divine tantrum!

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord

A timely example: Jack Welch just died. Welch ruined the GE company, which used to provide needed products at a fair price, and every year he arbitrarily fired 10% of the employees. If you admit the Christian definitions of God as the sole creator of the universe and the omnipotent cause of everything that happens in it, then you can't accept eternal damnation without convicting God of monstrous moral evil. Being omnipotent and omniscient, he didn't have to set things up that way!

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord
replies.content: The number of people who would freely choose to be tortured in unendurable misery for all eternity is probably quite small, you know. Jack Welch chooses evil; we're agreed on that. He doesn't choose a divine boot in the face forever.

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord

replies.title: Re: The Fear of the Lord
replies.content:
Dear JMG,

Thank you for your response. I agree with your reasoning re: hell. I am ashamed to admit it, but I went headlong into a very poorly thought out magical exercise by a certain supposed group of magicians who sell an ever-increasing library of books on Amazon. It involved a spirit - not a demon per se, but as I'm not all that literate magically, who knows? Afterward, due to my Christian upbringing, I began to feel badly about it and then frightened. For full disclosure, I unfortunately have OCD - which sometimes manifests as scrupulosity and attendant forms of catastrophizing. I had done the magical operation largely for two reasons (which I felt reasonable at the time): 1. If it worked, I'd have material evidence that the magical worldview was workable and "real." 2. P.S. I had a very vivid dream when I was 20 - a couple years before I began moving away from Christianity - in which I was dressed in dirty, colonial era clothing, and was very, very poor. My wife was dead. I had two small children to raise on my own. I took my children in an open cart pulled by a sickly looking gray horse or mule to a dry goods store to buy what I think was flour out of a small barrel. My son pointed to a small wooden toy horse up on a shelf and badly wanted it. I was so poor I could only afford the flour I had scooped out of the barrel into a smaller bag. I wanted so badly to buy him that horse. I woke up at that point BAWLING reaching my arms out to grab hold of my children and hug them. It took me a minute to realize I was a 20-year-old in the modern era, living in my own parents' house (at the time) and not an impoverished man in danger of starving to death with my children after having lost my wife. That *really* opened my mind to reincarnation.

JMG - thank you again for all you do and for your incredible generosity.

-Trepidation Nation

comments.title: Magical progress?
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
Is there progress in the accumulation of magical knowledge such that a Renaissance practitioner has a larger toolkit or a more effective toolkit than an an ancient Sumerian or a Paleolithic practitioner?

Or is it more a matter of different cultural lenses and maybe different priorities?

Thank You.

replies.title: Re: Magical progress?
replies.content:
Neither of those are the case, because you've missed a major historical fact. We know much less about magic than mages in the Renaissance did, and mages in the Renaissance knew much less about magic than mages in classical times did, because magic has been proscribed and persecuted for so much of the last fifteen hundred years or so. It's quite possible that magical knowledge accumulated from Sumerian to Roman times, but the arrival of Christianity and Islam brought about the loss of vast amounts of magical knowledge (and the killing of a great many insufficiently orthodox practitioners thereof).

During the Middle Ages and Renaissance, various groups of people in the Muslim and Christian worlds made serious efforts to reassemble as much as possible of what was lost, but the rise of religious fundamentalism and rationalist materialism thereafter brought those to an end and erased much of what had been done. When Eliphas Levi launched the modern magical revival in 1855 he was working with scraps and fragments only. Some good work has been done since then, but we're still nowhere near understanding the whole system of Renaissance magic, much less the fragments that survive of the magic of the ancients.

replies.title: Re: Magical progress?
replies.content: What about the magical traditions of the Far East?

replies.title: Re: Magical progress?
replies.content: They're much more complete, and that's why so many people in the West have done the hard work of learning Asian languages and going through the necessary process to become students of those traditions. Why do you think I studied t'ai chi for ten years, even at the cost of my health?

comments.title: Regardie
comments.content:
I was listening to Dr Tony Fuller recently talking about the Golden Dawn and he was quite contemptuous of Regardie saying that it was 'horrendous' that he spilled the beans on the Golden Dawn and then followed it up by saying that Regardie didn't know that much anyway as he never lasted that long in any magical order he was involved with.

"it is a huge disappointment that no effort - I repeat, no effort - was made to correct the numerous errors and significant omissions which appeared earlier. replies.title: Re: Regardie
replies.content:
Too funny. To begin with, Fuller is quite wrong -- I corrected dozens of errors, including misspelled Hebrew words and garbled passages in the rituals. I gather he didn't take the time to read the new edition with any particular care.

More broadly, though, the Golden Dawn was not a static tradition; the rituals and teachings were modified repeatedly over the history of the order. Regardie's book was a snapshot of the rituals at one particular point in the history of the Stella Matutina, the largest of the GD successor orders. As I explained in my foreword, the publisher and I jointly made the decision to bring out a clean and corrected version of Regardie's work as it was written, not to try to revise the text to fit any one of the dozens of earlier versions which some people consider more correct.

Finally, I take quite a bit of wry amusement in Fuller's insistence that he could have sent me a detailed list of corrections. I circulated an open letter throughout the GD community asking for help and feedback on the project; unless he was living under a rock, he saw the letter; and he could have sent me that list. He didn't, and neither did the others he mentions. The Golden Dawn scene is famous, and not in a good way, for the number of people in it who think that keeping copies of order papers locked up in their filing cabinets and refusing to share them with anyone else makes them somehow superior to the rest of us.

If Fuller thinks his version of the rituals and papers is better than Regardie's, fair enough -- let him publish them, so that students of the GD tradition can compare them with Regardie's. The rituals are long out of copyright; nothing stands in his way, and plenty of occult publishers would love to bring out something that interesting to students of the occult. What is he waiting for?

2) If the more complex version doesn't work for you, it doesn't. One of the reasons I created the simplified version is that a lot of people have trouble with the circulation of light.

3) The interesting thing here is that some of the old magical orders that use the SoP faced south, and some faced east. If you've been facing east, and it works for you, keep facing east.

replies.title: (no subject)
